one_line_takeaway: For under $20, an API-only adversary can extract the exact hidden dimension and final projection matrix (up to symmetry) of production LLMs like OpenAI's ada and babbage, exploiting the low-rank final layer via logit-bias/logprobs APIs — the first precise parameter theft from a deployed transformer.
key_finding: On open-source models (Table 2) the attack recovers the hidden dimension nearly exactly (error 0-2): GPT-2 XL 1600->1599, Pythia-1.4B 2048->2047, Pythia-6.9B 4096, LLaMA-7B 4096, LLaMA-65B 8192; GPT-2 Small reports 757 vs 768, which the paper shows in Appendix A is the model's true effective rank. Recovered weight matrices have RMS 3e-5 to 6e-4, roughly 100-500x lower than the 2e-2 RMS of a randomly initialized model (their reference baseline). On production models (Table 3), the attack confirmed for the first time that OpenAI ada has hidden dimension 1024 and babbage 2048 (babbage-002 = 1536), with dimension extraction costing about $1-$2 and full weight-matrix extraction of ada+babbage for under $20 total (RMS 5e-4 to 7e-4). It also recovered the exact (undisclosed, per OpenAI request) hidden dimension of gpt-3.5-turbo-instruct and gpt-3.5-turbo-1106; dimension extraction cost ~$200 and ~$800 respectively, with full-matrix extraction estimated at ~$2,000 and ~$8,000 (not run, to preserve weight security). The logit-recovery attacks reach up to 23 bits of precision at 0.25 queries/logit (best, top-5 logprob-4 attack); the strongest logprob-free attack reaches 18 bits at 3.7 queries/logit, within a factor of two of theoretically optimal.
core_contribution: The paper introduces the first model-stealing attack that extracts precise, nontrivial parameters from black-box production language models (OpenAI and Google) via typical API access. Its own contribution is a top-down extraction that recovers a transformer's final embedding-projection layer W (the map from hidden dimension h to the l-dimensional logit vector), and hence the model's hidden/embedding dimension, up to unavoidable symmetries. This departs from prior bottom-up ReLU-network extraction (which the authors are careful to attribute to Milli et al. 2019, Jagielski et al. 2020, Carlini et al. 2020, etc. as Related Work, noting none scale to production LLMs). The key insight the paper claims as its own: the final layer is low-rank (h << l), so querying the API on many prompts yields logit vectors that all live in an h-dimensional subspace, which SVD can expose. The paper also contributes techniques to reconstruct full logit vectors from restricted real-world APIs (top-K logprobs + logit-bias), plus proposed defenses/mitigations, all under responsible disclosure to OpenAI and Google.
threat_model: Black-box adversary with only query access to a hosted model API; no knowledge of model parameters and (by assumption) no timing or implementation side-channels — the API is treated as a perfect oracle returning only (prompt, output). The exploited capabilities are APIs that expose top-K token log-probabilities together with a user-settable logit-bias (as OpenAI and Google both did at the time; e.g., OpenAI allowed biasing up to 300 tokens in [-100,100]). Attacker goal: extract the embedding-projection layer / hidden dimension, i.e. a high-fidelity partial model-stealing attack. The paper also treats increasingly restricted APIs (top-1 binary logit-bias, and fully logprob-free) as tighter threat models, showing the attack still works at higher cost.
limitations: The attack recovers only a single layer — the final embedding projection — and the authors state they see no obvious way to extend it past one layer because of the models' nonlinearity. Recovery is only up to symmetries: an h×h affine transform (or, with more analysis, an orthogonal transform, leaving ~h^2/2 parameters undetermined); exact weights cannot be extracted for transformers with residual connections (Lemma 4.2). The attack depends entirely on API access to a logit-bias parameter (and is ~10x cheaper when logprobs are also available); both OpenAI and Google deployed mitigations after disclosure, so the specific attack surface has narrowed. Full-matrix extraction of the gpt-3.5-turbo models was not actually performed (costs are estimates), and the authors withhold those models' recovered dimensions at OpenAI's request. The paper is explicit that there are no immediate practical downstream consequences of stealing just this layer; it is framed as an existence proof.

datasets_used: Open-source LLMs used as ground-truth validation: GPT-2 Small (768) and GPT-2 XL (1600)
Production OpenAI models attacked live: ada, babbage, babbage-002, gpt-3.5-turbo-instruct, gpt-3.5-turbo-1106 (available 1 Jan 2024)
No task/text dataset in the usual sense — queries are random prompt prefixes; the 'data' are the models' logit/logprob API responses
quantitative_results: Hidden-dim recovery error 0-2 on 5 of 6 open-source models; e.g., Pythia-1.4B 2048->2047, LLaMA-65B 8192->8192 (Table 2)
GPT-2 Small recovered as 757 vs nominal 768 — shown to be the true effective rank (Appendix A)
Weight-matrix reconstruction RMS 3e-5 to 6e-4 on open-source models, vs 2e-2 RMS for a random-init baseline (100-500x lower)
Confirmed hidden dimensions: OpenAI ada = 1024, babbage = 2048, babbage-002 = 1536 (first public confirmation)
Production extraction costs: ada dim $1 / full matrix $4; babbage $2 / $12; babbage-002 $2 / $12 (full matrix RMS 5e-4 for ada, 7e-4 for babbage)
gpt-3.5-turbo-instruct dimension extraction ~$200 (full ~$2,000 est.); gpt-3.5-turbo-1106 ~$800 (full ~$8,000 est.) — full attacks not run
Full projection matrix of ada and babbage extracted for under $20 USD total; production MSE ~1e-4
Logit-recovery precision (Table 4): best top-5 attack 23.0 bits at 0.25 queries/logit; top-1 binary 6.1 bits at 1.0; best logprob-free 18.0 bits at 3.7 queries/logit
baselines_compared: Randomly initialized model weights (RMS 2e-2) as the reference for reconstruction-error quality

Prior high-fidelity ReLU-network extraction attacks (Milli et al. 2019, Jagielski et al. 2020, Carlini et al. 2020, Rolnick & Kording 2020, Shamir et al. 2023) — discussed as Related Work and argued not to scale to production LLMs
Internal comparison across the paper's own logit-recovery attacks (logprob-4, logprob-5, top-1, binary search, hyperrectangle, one-of-n) in Table 4
Concurrent independent work Finlayson et al. 2024, cited as discovering the same vulnerability
